source.title: Neuropsychological evidence for multiple implicit memory systems: a comparison of Alzheimer's, Huntington's, and Parkinson's disease patients.

confidence_rationale: Classic double-dissociation study across three patient groups (DAT, HD, PD). The key finding for MECH-172: DAT patients are severely impaired on explicit memory but show NORMAL motor skill learning on the pursuit-rotor task AND normal performance on the weight-biasing task -- the same tasks on which HD patients (with striatal pathology) are specifically impaired. The dissociation is the double: DAT (hippocampal) impairs declarative but spares motor skill/procedural; HD (striatal) impairs motor skill/procedural but spares declarative. This directly demonstrates the architectural separation predicted by MECH-172 (habit/procedural memory is spared in early AD because it depends on a corticostriatal system that does not require offline consolidation).
failure_signatures: Motor learning tasks (pursuit-rotor, weight-biasing) are preserved in DAT but lexical priming is also impaired in DAT -- the sparing is specific to motor procedural learning, not all implicit memory.
Small sample sizes by modern standards; early clinical diagnostic criteria for DAT.
Does not directly test whether preservation is due to 'no offline consolidation required' vs. some other mechanism of striatal independence.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Dementia of the Alzheimer type (DAT) patients perform normally on pursuit-rotor motor learning and weight-biasing tasks (both implicit procedural/motor tasks) but are severely impaired on explicit memory. Huntington's disease patients show the reverse: impaired on motor learning tasks but relatively spared on lexical priming. This double dissociation indicates that motor skill learning is mediated by a corticostriatal system distinct from the hippocampal-neocortical declarative memory system.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-172 predicts that habit/procedural memory is architecturally spared in early AD because it depends on a model-free corticostriatal habit system that does not require offline consolidation phases. The declarative (episodic/contextual) attribution pipeline requires NREM/REM offline phases; the corticostriatal habit system does not. This paper provides the foundational neuropsychological evidence for that architectural separation: DAT selectively damages the hippocampal system (pipeline substrate) while leaving the striatal habit system intact. The double dissociation with HD confirms the systems are genuinely neurologically separable.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper establishes the neuropsychological dissociation at the system level; it does not test whether the preservation is because no offline consolidation is required (the MECH-172 mechanism) vs. simple anatomical separation. The claim that corticostriatal habit learning requires no offline consolidation is supported by adjacent literature but not tested here directly.
mapping.source_context: Clinical neuropsychology study at San Diego VA; DAT, HD, and demented/nondemented PD patients; pursuit-rotor motor task (200 ms target) and weight-biasing task; explicit recall and lexical priming controls.

confidence_components.notes: Classic paper, highly cited, double-dissociation design. Mapping fidelity high -- the architectural separation is exactly what MECH-172 predicts. Transfer risk low -- directly tests AD patients on procedural vs. declarative tasks. Confidence limited by small samples and older diagnostic criteria.
